items.q: Is one leadership style always best?
items.correct: No — the effective style depends on the situation: task urgency, the skill and experience of staff, and the culture; good leaders adapt their style.
items.misconception: Democratic leadership is always superior to autocratic leadership.
items.random_wrong: UK companies must register their leadership style with Companies House.
items.why: Students rank styles as good or bad, missing the contingency view that context determines effectiveness.

items.q: Do decision trees guarantee the right decision?
items.correct: No — they clarify options and expected values, but probabilities and payoffs are estimates, and they ignore qualitative factors like morale and brand image.
items.misconception: Following the highest expected value branch ensures the best real outcome.
items.random_wrong: Decision trees are only used by businesses in the forestry industry.
items.why: Students treat estimated probabilities as facts; expected value is a long-run average, not a guarantee for a one-off decision.

items.q: Are managers and leaders the same thing?
items.correct: Not exactly — management focuses on planning, organising and controlling resources, while leadership is about setting direction and inspiring people; one person may do both.
items.misconception: Anyone with a manager job title is automatically a leader.
items.random_wrong: Leaders are elected by employees under company law.
items.why: Students conflate the formal role with the influencing skill, a distinction this topic examines directly.

items.q: In a decision tree, the expected value of an option is found by:
items.correct: Multiplying each outcome's payoff by its probability and summing the results
items.misconception: Choosing the highest payoff regardless of probability
items.random_wrong: Adding all the payoffs and dividing by the costs
items.why: It's Multiplying each outcome's payoff by its probability and summing the results. “Choosing the highest payoff regardless of probability” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: Makes decisions alone with little consultation
items.misconception: Delegates most decisions to the team
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Makes decisions alone with little consultation. “Delegates most decisions to the team” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Mendelow's matrix maps stakeholders according to their:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Power and interest. “Profit and loss” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Intuitive decision-making is most appropriate when:
items.correct: Time is short, data is limited and the manager has deep experience
items.misconception: Large, reliable data sets are readily available
items.random_wrong: The decision is so high-risk that documented evidence is essential
items.why: It's Time is short, data is limited and the manager has deep experience. “Large, reliable data sets are readily available” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Option A has a 0.6 probability of £50,000 profit and a 0.4 probability of a £10,000 loss. What is its expected value in £?
items.why: Exact answer: 26000 £. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
